1: anechthe (anago) — led up — the passive (was led up) makes the Spirit the agent of the wilderness-leading, just as YHWH led Israel in the wilderness (Deuteronomy 8:2, the immediate context of Jesus's first temptation response). peirasthēnai — to be tested/tempted — peirazo carries both meanings: external testing (to prove quality) and internal temptation (to induce sin). The wilderness testing recapitulates Israel's 40 years (Jesus's 40 days) with the key difference that Jesus passes where Israel failed.
17: apo tote erxato — from that time he began — apo tote is a Matthean structural marker used at key transitions (also 16:21 for the passion-announcements). kerusso — to herald, proclaim — the verb of public announcement rather than private teaching; Jesus speaks as one with royal authority proclaiming the King's news. The message is identical to John's (Matthew 3:2): metanoeite, engiken gar he basileia ton ouranon — but Jesus proclaims it as the one in whom the kingdom is present, not merely approaching.
2: Nēsteusas hēmeras tessarakonta kai nyktas tessarakonta — having fasted forty days and forty nights — the dual formula (days and nights) precisely parallels Moses's Sinai fast (Exodus 34:28; Deuteronomy 9:9, 18: both forty days and forty nights) and Elijah's wilderness fast (1 Kings 19:8: forty days and forty nights to Horeb). Jesus recapitulates both figures. Husteron epeinasen — afterward he was hungry — the aorist epeinasen (he became hungry) is the physiological trigger: genuine human hunger is the entry-point for the tempter. The forty-day fast is completed; the vulnerability is real.
3: Ei huios ei tou theou — if you are the Son of God — ei with present indicative is a first-class condition assuming the reality: "since you are the Son of God..." The tempter does not deny the sonship but attempts to redirect it toward self-service. Eipe hina hoi lithoi houtoi artoi genōntai — tell these stones to become bread — the aorist subjunctive genōntai (become) requests a creative word, a use of divine-son power for immediate physical self-provision. The temptation is to deploy the power of sonship for private benefit rather than for the mission to which the Father has called.
4: Gegraptai — it is written, it stands written — the perfect passive of graphō (to write): the scripture has been written and its authority remains in effect. Jesus's triple use of gegraptai (Matthew 4:4, 7, 10) positions his responses as authoritative appeals to standing divine word, not ad hoc arguments. The Deuteronomy 8:3 citation: ouk ep' artō monō zēsetai ho anthrōpos — man shall not live by bread alone — directly addresses Israel's wilderness murmuring for food; Jesus, unlike Israel, trusts the Father's provision and does not demand bread from stone.
5: Tēn hagian polin — the holy city — Matthew's distinctive designation for Jerusalem (also Matthew 27:53), reflecting the city's sanctified cultic status. Pterygion tou hierou — the pinnacle/parapet of the temple — pterygion (diminutive of pteryx, wing) = wing, parapet, or projecting edge; used for the highest point of the temple complex. According to Josephus (Antiquities 15.11.5), the southeast corner of the temple platform overlooks a sheer drop of several hundred feet into the Kidron Valley — a height sufficient to make the test of angelic protection dramatically visible.
6: The devil cites Psalms 91:11-12 (LXX 90): tois angelois autou enteleitai peri sou — he will command his angels concerning you — but omits the clause "to guard you in all your ways" that follows in the Psalm. The omission strips the promise from its covenantal context (the path of covenant obedience) to make it a basis for presumption (demanding miraculous rescue to prove God's care). Jesus's counter (Deuteronomy 6:16) identifies this as "putting the Lord to the test" — exactly the misuse of divine promise that the Psalm's context guards against.
7: Gegraptai palin — it is written again — Jesus meets the devil's scripture-citation with another scripture-citation, demonstrating that the devil's use of Ps 91 requires correction by Deuteronomy 6:16, not by denying the Psalm. Ouk ekpeiraseis kyrion ton theon sou — you shall not put the Lord your God to the test — Deuteronomy 6:16 explicitly cites the Massah incident (Exodus 17:1-7) where Israel demanded water as a test of God's presence. The verb ekpeiraseis (future indicative, from ekpeirazō) means to put to an extreme test, to demand proof by creating a crisis. Jesus refuses to engineer a rescue to prove the Father's faithfulness.
8: Oros hypsēlon lian — a very high mountain — lian (very, exceedingly) intensifies the height beyond any natural geography. The mountain may be visionary or apocalyptic rather than a specific physical peak — Matthew does not identify it. Pasas tas basileias tou kosmou kai tēn doxan autōn — all the kingdoms of the world and their glory — pasas (all, every) and doxan (glory, splendor, magnificence) make the offer genuinely comprehensive. Whether the vision was literal or representative, Matthew presents Jesus's response as treating the offer as real — which gives his refusal its full moral weight.
9: Tauta soi panta dōsō ean pesōn proskunēsēs moi — all these I will give to you if having fallen you worship me — the conditional ean with aorist subjunctive presents a single, decisive act: one moment of submission. Pesōn (having fallen) describes the prostration of worship; combined with proskunēsēs (do obeisance, worship) the action is unmistakable. The verb proskyneō is used throughout Matthew for worship of Jesus (Matthew 2:11; Matthew 8:2; Matthew 14:33; Matthew 28:9, 17) and for the proper worship of God alone (Matthew 4:10) — the devil demands the specific act reserved for God.
10: Hypage, Satana — go away, Satan — the imperative hypage (depart, go away) is authoritative dismissal; the naming Satana (transliteration of Hebrew Satan, the Adversary) identifies the tempter definitively for the first time in the exchange. The name will recur at Peter's rebuke (Matthew 16:23: "Hypage opiso mou, Satana" — the verbatim repetition there is deliberate). The Deuteronomy 6:13 citation: Kyrion ton theon sou proskunēseis kai autō monō latreueis — you shall worship the Lord your God and him alone you shall serve — the emphatic monō (alone, only) is not in the MT but appears in Matthew's text.
11: Kai idou angeloi prosēlthon kai diēkonoun autō — and behold, angels came to him and were serving/ministering to him — idou (behold!) signals a significant moment. Prosēlthon (aorist: came to, approached) is followed by the imperfect diēkonoun (were serving, ministering — continuous action): the angels provide sustained care, not a single act. Diakonein (to serve, minister) is the same word for table-service (Matthew 8:15; Matthew 25:44; Matthew 27:55) — the angels provide physical care (food and rest?) after the extended fast. The episode recalls Elijah's angelic refreshment after his own wilderness fast (1 Kings 19:5-8).
12: Akousas de hoti Iōannēs paredothē — but having heard that John was handed over — paradidōmi (to hand over, deliver up) is Matthew's term for betrayal and arrest (Matthew 17:22; Matthew 20:18; Matthew 26:2, 15, 21, 23, 24, 25, 45, 46, 48); its use for John's arrest prefigures the same fate for Jesus. Anechōrēsen eis tēn Galilaian — he withdrew into Galilee — anachoreo (to withdraw, retire strategically) is used throughout Matthew 2 for divinely-guided repositioning; its reappearance here frames Jesus's Galilean move as theologically intentional rather than a retreat from danger.
13: Katōkēsen eis Kapharnaoum tēn paraliian — he took up residence in Capernaum by the sea — katōkēsen (aorist of katoikeo, to settle, take up permanent residence — stronger than temporary lodging) indicates Capernaum became Jesus's home base for the Galilean ministry. The descriptor paraliia (coastal, by the sea) locates Capernaum on the shore of the Sea of Galilee, important for the following Isaianic citation's mention of "the Way of the Sea." En horiois Zaboulōn kai Nephaleim — in the regions of Zebulun and Naphtali — the tribal territories frame the prophetic fulfillment.
14: Hina plērōthē to rhēthen dia Ēsaiou tou prophētou — so that what was spoken through Isaiah the prophet might be fulfilled — the fifth hina plērōthē fulfillment formula in Matthew 1-4 (Matthew 1:22; Matthew 2:15, 23; Matthew 4:14), each marking a new stage in the Christological fulfillment sequence. Legontos (present participle = who says) treats Isaiah's oracle as a living word still speaking — the prophet's voice is not past tense but present, awaiting its moment of actualization. The formula signals to Matthew's readers that this geographic relocation is not accidental but prophetically determined.
15: The Isaiah 9:1 citation: Gē Zaboulōn kai gē Nephaleim — land of Zebulun and land of Naphtali — the territories of the northern tribes, first to fall to Assyria in 732 BCE (2 Kings 15:29). Hodos thalassēs, peran tou Iordanou — the Way of the Sea, beyond the Jordan — hodos thalassēs is the ancient trade and military road running along the Mediterranean coast and through the Jordan valley (the Via Maris of Latin tradition). Galilaia tōn ethnōn — Galilee of the Gentiles — the mixed-population region where Gentiles lived alongside Jews, appropriate for a ministry that will eventually extend to all nations (Matthew 28:19).
16: Ho laos ho kathēmenos en skotia phōs eiden mega — the people sitting in darkness saw a great light — the present participle kathēmenos (sitting, dwelling) describes a state of settled habitation in darkness, not merely passing through it. Phōs eiden mega — they saw a great light — the aorist eiden is punctiliar: a decisive, singular moment of seeing. Tois en chōra kai skia thanatou — to those in the region and shadow of death — skia thanatou (shadow of death) is a compound expression found in Psalms 22:4; Matthew 23:4 (LXX); Job 3:5 for the deepest darkness. Isaiah's oracle of reversal — darkness to light — is fulfilled in Jesus's Galilean ministry.
18: Amfiblēstron — a casting net — the specific net-type (amphiblestron, a net thrown so it spreads in a circle and closes like a bag around the fish) distinguishes from the dragnet (sagēnē, Matthew 13:47) used from a boat. The present participle ballontas (throwing, casting) indicates the brothers were actively at work fishing when Jesus encountered them — their occupation was fully engaged, making the call's interruption and their response more striking. Hēsan gar halieis — for they were fishermen — the explanatory imperfect identifies their occupation and qualifications for the metaphor Jesus will use.
19: Deute opisō mou — come after me — the imperative deute (come!, plural) combined with opisō mou (behind me, after me) is the standard teacher-disciple relationship formula: to come after a rabbi was to follow in his path, learn his way, and eventually replicate his teaching. Poiēsō hymas halieis anthrōpōn — I will make you fishers of people — the word-play on their occupation: the same halieuo (to fish) now describes their mission. The metaphor is active: fishing requires skill, effort, and going to where the fish are.
20: Hoi de eutheos aphentes ta diktya ēkolouthēsan autō — they immediately left the nets and followed him — eutheos (immediately, at once) is a Matthean marker of decisive, unhesitating response. Aphentes (having left/abandoned) carries the weight of real cost: the nets (their livelihood and family business equipment) are left behind. Ēkolouthēsan autō — they followed him — the aorist of akoloutheo (to follow, accompany in discipleship) is used throughout Matthew for committed following (Matthew 8:22-23; Matthew 9:9; Matthew 10:38; Matthew 16:24; Matthew 19:21, 27, 28).
21: Katartizontas ta diktya auton — mending/repairing their nets — the present participle katartizontas (from katartizō, to restore, mend, put in order) indicates the ongoing work of net repair between fishing expeditions — a normal part of the fisher's routine. The verb also means "to equip" or "make complete" (used in Ephesians 4:12 for equipping the saints; 1 Corinthians 1:10 for being united in the same mind). James and John are at work in the boat with their father Zebedee (meta Zebedaiou tou patros auton) — the family business context frames the cost of the call.
22: Aphentes to ploion kai ton patera auton — leaving the boat and their father — the objects left by James and John include both property (the boat, the family business) and family relationships (their father). Matthew's parallel structure (Peter and Andrew / James and John) is deliberate: the two pairs are called in identical circumstances and respond identically (eutheos/aphentes/ēkolouthēsan = immediately/having left/followed). The escalating cost (nets → nets + boat + father) is highlighted by the parallel construction.
23: Periēgen en holē tē Galilaia — he was going about in all of Galilee — the imperfect periēgen (was going about, was traveling through) indicates an extended, ongoing campaign throughout the region. Three simultaneous activities described by present participles: didaskon (teaching, in the synagogues), kēryssōn (proclaiming the gospel of the kingdom), and therapeuōn (healing every disease and every infirmity). Pasan noson kai pasan malakian — every disease and every weakness/infirmity — the double pasan (every) emphasizes comprehensive scope; malakia (weakness, infirmity) covers conditions that are not strictly diseases.
24: Apēlthen hē akoē autou eis holēn tēn Syrian — the report/news of him went out into all of Syria — akoē (report, news, hearing) is the word for what is heard about someone; the phrase marks the first explicit geographic extension of Jesus's fame beyond Jewish territory to the Roman province of Syria (north and east of Galilee). Daimonizoumenous kai selēniazomenous kai paralytikous — those possessed by demons and those who were moonstruck (epileptics?) and those who were paralyzed — the three categories cover demonic affliction, neurological/psychiatric conditions, and physical paralysis.
25: Ēkolouthēsan autō ochloi polloi — great crowds followed him — the verb akoloutheo (to follow) appears again, now applied to crowds rather than disciples; in Matthew the word carries both levels of meaning (physical accompanying and committed discipleship). The five geographic sources — apo tēs Galilaias kai Dekapoleōs kai Ierosolymōn kai Ioudaias kai peran tou Iordanou — cover virtually all the Jewish population regions: the Galilean heartland, the Gentile Decapolis, the capital and its province, and Transjordan (Perea). The gathering from all directions sets the stage for the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5:1-2).
